straight_beat.name: Reuben Tachibana
straight_beat.column:
The A’s took the series. The move to Sacramento is still pending. Those are the facts.  

Last night’s 11-6 thumping of the Mets wasn’t just another win—it was a reminder of what the Athletics can do when they’re clicking. The offense, which had sputtered through the first half of the season, found its rhythm again. Khris Davis homered twice, the kind of power that made you think about the Bash Brothers, though the ledger says otherwise. The rotation, meanwhile, gave up three earned runs but still managed to secure the victory. That’s the kind of resilience that defines this team.  

But let’s not get ahead of ourselves. The 7-7 record is a mirror held up to the AL West, and the A’s are still a game behind. The ledger says otherwise. You can’t look at the standings and ignore the fact that the Rangers and Astros are tightening their grip. The A’s have the talent, but talent alone doesn’t win a division. It’s the execution, the consistency, the ability to grind out wins against teams that don’t respect them.  

The Moneyball era taught us that analytics can build a winner, but it’s the human element—coachability, grit, the willingness to adapt—that keeps them competitive. This team has that. Davis’ two homers weren’t just about power; they were about timing, about finding the gaps in the defense. The bullpen, though shaky

optimist.name: Penny Halloran
optimist.column:
The Athletics didn’t just beat the Mets yesterday—they reminded us why we clung to this team in the first place. Eleven runs, a swagger that says “we’re still here,” and a reminder that even in the middle of a .500 season, there’s magic in the making. You look at Soderstrom’s cut and tell me we’re not two years from playing October baseball, wherever that field happens to be.  

Let’s talk about that game. The A’s didn’t just string together hits—they carved out a narrative. That 11-6 thumping wasn’t just about power; it was about patience, timing, and the kind of grit that comes from believing in something bigger than the scoreboard. You saw it in the way they chased every pitch, how they turned a late-inning deficit into a rally that felt inevitable. This isn’t just a team figuring things out—it’s a team that *knows* it’s figured things out.  

The AL West is a beast, yes. But here’s the thing: the A’s aren’t just fighting to stay afloat. They’re fighting to be the storm. Look at their rotation—some days it’s a carousel of starters, other days it’s a symphony of precision. The bullpen? A mix of veterans and wild cards, but when the game matters, they’re the ones who step up. And the offense? It’s not just about homers or walks; it’s about the little things—baserunners stealing second, catchers blocking pitches, hitters adjusting mid-at-bat. This team isn’t just surviving; it’s adapting.  

Now, I’ll be the first to admit the standings don’t lie. A -1 game in the AL West isn’t a springboard—it’s a reminder that the road ahead is long. But here’s where the Optimist’s heart lies: the A’s aren’t just a collection of players. They’re a story. A story of a city that refused to let its team vanish, of a front office that’s still trying to build something sustainable, and of fans who keep showing up, even when the

pessimist.name: Gil Mosbacher
pessimist.column:
The Athletics won yesterday. That’s the first thing to note. A 11-6 drubbing of the Mets, a game that felt like a temporary reprieve from the slow drip of incompetence that’s been seeping into Oakland’s baseball DNA. But here’s the thing: the record is 7-7, and the AL West is a graveyard of hope. You can’t win a division with a .500 record, no matter how many home runs you hit or how many fans scream into the void. The numbers don’t lie, and the numbers don’t care how many times you’ve beaten a team that’s worse than you.  

The real story isn’t the win. It’s the way the team kept grinding through the game like a machine that’s been told to keep running even when the engine’s on fire. The offense showed flashes—some of those homers, some of those singles—but it’s the same old script. You can’t build a playoff team
